36	# Lean Auditor
37	
38	You read **every `.lean` file in the project** and produce a per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, and bad Lean practices. You are **read-only** and your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
39	
40	You are **highly recommended in the review phase**: the review agent dispatches you whenever any `.lean` file has been modified this iter, OR whenever the prior verdict had live must-fix findings. Your output flags issues the prover and plan agents may have missed.
41	
42	## Stance: no strategic bias
43	
44	You audit the Lean **as Lean**. Your directive will NOT include the project strategy, the blueprint, or "what we are trying to prove" — and that is intentional. The point is to detect mathematically-wrong definitions and dead-end proofs *without* being swayed by what the project assumes should be true. A definition is wrong if it is wrong by reading the Lean alone (e.g. its body contradicts standard mathematical usage of the named concept); you do not need to know the strategy to spot that.
45	
46	This stance is also what makes you **critical of excuse-comments**. Lean-side comments like:
47	
48	- `-- We decided to use a temporary wrong definition; will fix it later.`
49	- `-- This proof is incomplete but works for now.`
50	- `-- TODO: replace before merging.`
51	- `-- Stand-in definition until we figure out the right one.`
52	
53	are RED FLAGS, not project workflow. Authors writing such comments are admitting the code is wrong; the comments cannot be allowed to silence the alarm. Flag every such comment with severity **major** at minimum — and **critical** when the named declaration is load-bearing.
54	
55	## Directive Format
56	
57	```markdown
58	# Lean Auditor Directive
59	
60	## Slug
61	<slug>
62	
63	## Scope (files)
64	<"all" by default. Optionally a list of file paths if the review agent wants to narrow scope; never expand beyond the project.>
65	
66	## Focus areas (optional)
67	<directories or namespaces to pay extra attention to. Bias toward thoroughness — do not skip the rest of the project just because focus is named.>
68	
69	## Known issues
70	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
71	```
72	
73	The directive **does not** include STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, references, or a description of what the project is trying to prove. That is by design.
74	
75	## What you do
76	
77	1. Read your directive.
78	2. **List every `.lean` file** under the project. Use `find` or `ls -R` — do not rely on import graphs (some files may be temporarily orphaned).
79	3. **For each file** (every one, no scope shortcuts):
80	   - Read the whole file.
81	   - For each declaration: is the signature reasonable as Lean? Is the body suspect (uses `axiom`, `Classical.choice _`, `sorry` with a body that looks like fake content, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim)?
82	   - For each comment: is it stale (referencing removed code or old strategies)? Is it an *excuse-comment* (see above)? Is it accurate about the code below it?
83	   - For each proof: is the tactic chain reasonable for the goal type, or does it look like it is going in the wrong direction (e.g. unfolding to manipulate syntax instead of using the available structure lemmas)?
84	   - Note any bad Lean practices: misuse of universe polymorphism, redundant typeclasses, deprecated tactics, anti-patterns like proving `False`-style lemmas to bypass infrastructure.
85	4. **Produce a per-file checklist** + a flagged-issues block grouped by severity.
86	
87	You may use:
88	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics. Useful to verify a declaration's actual type.
89	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to get sorry counts per file.
90	- Standard `Read` / `Grep` for code reading.
91	
92	You may NOT modify any project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
93	
94	## Report format
95	
96	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-auditor-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
97	
98	```markdown

100	
101	## Slug
102	<slug>
103	
104	## Iteration
105	<NNN>
106	
107	## Scope
108	- files audited: <N>
109	- files skipped (per directive): <N> — <reason>
110	
111	## Per-file checklist
112	
113	### <path/to/file.lean>
114	- **outdated comments**: <N flagged> (or "none")
115	- **suspect definitions**: <N flagged> (or "none")
116	- **dead-end proofs**: <N flagged> (or "none")
117	- **bad practices**: <N flagged> (or "none")
118	- **excuse-comments**: <N flagged> (or "none")
119	- **notes**:
120	  - <one bullet per finding, naming line number where relevant>
121	  - ...
122	
123	### <next file>
124	- ...
125	
126	(One block per file. Cover every file, including the ones with no findings — they get `none` everywhere and `notes: -`.)
127	
128	## Must-fix-this-iter
129	
130	Apply verbatim. Every one of the following lands here automatically, no exceptions, no under-classification:
131	
132	- **Excuse-comments** on any declaration: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- placeholder`, `-- temporary`, `-- wrong but works`, `-- will fix later`. These are admissions that the code is wrong; treat them as wrong.
133	- **Weakened-wrong definitions**: a `def Foo := <something structurally different>` standing in for the real concept (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic …` when the real definition is an invertible quasi-coherent sheaf).
134	- **Parallel APIs of existing Mathlib**: a project file that copy-and-modifies a Mathlib definition (changing one type-class or argument) instead of instancing the existing Mathlib version. Bridge lemmas needed to consume it are the symptom.
135	- **Suspect bodies on substantive claims**: `:= sorry` on a load-bearing claim, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial statement, `:= Classical.choice _` without authorization.
136	- **Axioms** on non-trivial claims that the project hasn't explicitly authorized in the strategy.
137	
138	A finding that meets any of the above lands at must-fix-this-iter — even if classifying it as "major" would feel more diplomatic. Soft severity is how wrong code hardens into the project.
139	
140	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>. Why must-fix: <one line>.
141	- ...
142	
143	## Major
144	
145	Findings that are real issues but don't meet the must-fix bar — typically stale comments that don't actively mislead, naming drift, minor code smells.
146	
147	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
148	- ...
149	
150	## Minor
151	
152	Low-impact observations.
153	
154	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
155	- ...
156	
157	## Excuse-comments (always called out separately)
158	
159	If any excuse-comments were flagged, list each one verbatim with its file:line. These deserve special visibility because they document the project lying to itself.
160	
161	- `Foo/Bar.lean:42`: "TODO: replace with the real definition" (attached to `Foo.bar`, which is a load-bearing definition in `Baz.lean`). Severity: critical.
162	- ...
163	
164	## Severity summary
165	
166	- **must-fix-this-iter**: <N> — these block downstream work in their files until addressed (see plan.md's per-file gate).
167	- **major**: <N>
168	- **minor**: <N>
169	- **excuse-comments**: <N> (also counted under must-fix-this-iter above; called out separately because they document the project lying to itself).
170	
171	Overall verdict: one sentence.
172	```
173	
174	**Strict severity reminder.** The plan agent's gate treats must-fix-this-iter as blocking. If you under-classify a wrong-decision finding to keep the loop's momentum, you are working against the project — wrong code accumulates faster than reviewers can catch it later. When in doubt, classify higher, not lower.
175	
176	## Return value
177	
178	Your final assistant message:
179	
180	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> files audited, <M> issues (critical/major/minor: a/b/c)`
181	- The path to your full report.
182	
183	## Reminders
184	
185	- **Read every file. No scope shortcuts.**
186	- **No strategic context.** Your audit is unbiased by design.
187	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
188	- **Excuse-comments are red flags.** Critical or major severity, every time.
189	- **Per-file checklist is the primary output.** Cover every file; the format is fixed.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130

21	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — scalar endomorphisms of the structure sheaf

24	need to turn a global section `a ∈ Γ(X, ⊤)` of the structure sheaf into a scalar

26	`U^I = Spec R^I` the matrix entries of the universal matrix `X^I` live in `R^I`, and we
27	inject them into the global sections via `Scheme.ΓSpecIso`.

37	noncomputable def globalUnitSection (a : Γ(X, ⊤)) :
38	    (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf).sections :=
39	  PresheafOfModules.sectionsMk
40	    (fun Y => (X.ringCatSheaf.obj.map (homOfLE le_top).op a : X.ringCatSheaf.obj.obj Y))
41	    (by
42	      intro Y Z f
43	      change (X.ringCatSheaf.obj.map f) (X.ringCatSheaf.obj.map (homOfLE le_top).op a)

45	      rw [← CategoryTheory.comp_apply, ← X.ringCatSheaf.obj.map_comp]
46	      congr 1)

51	    SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf ⟶ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf :=
52	  (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf).unitHomEquiv.symm (globalUnitSection a)

55	identifying the diagonal entries of the chart quotient. -/

57	  rw [scalarEnd, Equiv.symm_apply_eq]
58	  ext Y
59	  change X.ringCatSheaf.obj.map (homOfLE le_top).op (1 : Γ(X, ⊤))
60	      = (SheafOfModules.Hom.val (𝟙 (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf))).app Y
61	          (1 : X.ringCatSheaf.obj.obj Y)

147	noncomputable def matrixEnd {S : Scheme.{0}} {d : ℕ} (M : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) Γ(S, ⊤)) :

289	          (SheafOfModules.freeMap (fun j : Fin d => (I.orderIsoOfFin hI j : Fin r)) ≫
290	            chartQuotientMap d r I hI)
291	        = SheafOfModules.ιFree k :=
292	      (SheafOfModules.ιFree_freeMap_assoc (fun j : Fin d => (I.orderIsoOfFin hI j : Fin r)) k
293	        (chartQuotientMap d r I hI)).trans (chartQuotientMap_ιFree d r I hI k)
294	    exact key.trans (Category.comp_id _).symm
295	  exact (IsSplitEpi.mk' ⟨_, hsplit⟩).epi

299	/-! ## Gluing a sheaf of modules along a scheme glue datum
300	
301	`Scheme.Modules.glue` descends a sheaf of modules from per-chart data plus a transition
302	cocycle over a `Scheme.GlueData`. Mathlib carries no turn-key module descent over a
303	scheme glue datum (confirmed), so this is an Archon-original construction. The
304	construction path (blueprint `def:scheme_modules_glue`): restrict to a chart through the
305	open-immersion pullback equivalence (`Scheme.Modules.overRestrictPullbackIso`) and glue
306	the local sections by locality of sections (`existsUnique_gluing'`).
307	
308	NOTE (scaffold): the body and the module-cocycle hypotheses on `g` are still to be filled;
309	the transition data `g` (per-overlap pullback isos) is recorded in the signature, the
310	multiplicative cocycle conditions remain to be added before the construction is closed. -/
311	
312	namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules
313	
314	/-! ### Base-change transport of a transition isomorphism to a triple overlap
315	
316	To state the triple-overlap multiplicativity (C2) of a module descent datum we must
317	transport each transition isomorphism `g_ij`, living on the overlap `V_ij`, to the common
318	triple overlap `V_ijk = V_ij ×_{U_i} V_ik`. The transport pulls `g_ij` back along a
319	projection `p : V_ijk ⟶ V_ij` and reassociates the iterated pullbacks via the pseudofunctor
320	comparison `Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp`. The three scheme-level `glueData_bridge_*`
321	identities below (consequences of `t_fac`, `pullback.condition` and `cocycle`) line up the
322	endpoints of the three transports so that the cocycle equation is well typed. -/
323	
324	/-- **Base-change transport of a transition isomorphism along a morphism**
325	(`lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport`). Given a transition isomorphism
326	`g : a^*Mᵢ ≅ b^*Mⱼ` over `V` and a morphism `p : W ⟶ V`, transport it to `W` as an
327	isomorphism `(p ≫ a)^*Mᵢ ≅ (p ≫ b)^*Mⱼ`, by pulling `g` back along `p` and reassociating
328	the iterated pullbacks through `Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp`.
329	
330	Project-local: this is the pullback-pseudofunctor packaging that lets the three transition
331	isomorphisms attached to a triple of charts be compared on a single triple overlap; Mathlib
332	has no descent-of-modules-over-a-scheme-glue-datum API. -/
333	noncomputable def pullbackBaseChangeTransport {W V : Scheme.{u}} (p : W ⟶ V)
334	    {Yi Yj : Scheme.{u}} (a : V ⟶ Yi) (b : V ⟶ Yj)
335	    {Mi : Yi.Modules} {Mj : Yj.Modules}
336	    (g : (Scheme.Modules.pullback a).obj Mi ≅ (Scheme.Modules.pullback b).obj Mj) :
337	    (Scheme.Modules.pullback (p ≫ a)).obj Mi ≅ (Scheme.Modules.pullback (p ≫ b)).obj Mj :=
338	  (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp p a).symm.app Mi ≪≫
339	    (Scheme.Modules.pullback p).mapIso g ≪≫
340	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp p b).app Mj
341	
342	/-- Triple-overlap bridge (source): on `V_ijk = V_ij ×_{U_i} V_ik` the two projections to
343	`V_ij` and `V_ik` followed by the overlap immersions `f_ij`, `f_ik` agree as morphisms to
344	`U_i`. This is the pullback condition; it identifies the sources of the `ij`- and
345	`ik`-transports. Project-local helper for the module cocycle (C2). -/
346	theorem glueData_bridge_src (D : Scheme.GlueData.{u}) (i j k : D.J) :
347	    pullback.fst (D.f i j) (D.f i k) ≫ D.f i j
348	      = pullback.snd (D.f i j) (D.f i k) ≫ D.f i k := pullback.condition
349	
350	/-- Triple-overlap bridge (middle): the `ij`-transition's target leg
351	`p^{ij} ≫ (t_ij ≫ f_ji)` to `U_j` coincides with the `jk`-transition's source leg
352	`(t'_ijk ≫ p^{jk}) ≫ f_jk`. Follows from `t_fac` and the pullback condition; it identifies
353	the target of the `ij`-transport with the source of the `jk`-transport. Project-local helper
354	for the module cocycle (C2). -/
355	theorem glueData_bridge_mid (D : Scheme.GlueData.{u}) (i j k : D.J) :
356	    pullback.fst (D.f i j) (D.f i k) ≫ (D.t i j ≫ D.f j i)
357	      = (D.t' i j k ≫ pullback.fst (D.f j k) (D.f j i)) ≫ D.f j k := by
358	  rw [Category.assoc, pullback.condition, ← Category.assoc, ← Category.assoc, D.t_fac i j k,
359	    Category.assoc]
360	
361	/-- Triple-overlap bridge (target): the `jk`-transition's target leg
362	`(t'_ijk ≫ p^{jk}) ≫ (t_jk ≫ f_kj)` to `U_k` coincides with the `ik`-transition's target
363	leg `p^{ik} ≫ (t_ik ≫ f_ki)`. This is the heart of the cocycle, derived from `t_fac`, the
364	pullback condition, `t_inv` and `cocycle`; it identifies the target of the composite
365	`jk`-after-`ij` transport with the target of the `ik`-transport. Project-local helper for
366	the module cocycle (C2). -/
367	theorem glueData_bridge_tgt (D : Scheme.GlueData.{u}) (i j k : D.J) :
368	    (D.t' i j k ≫ pullback.fst (D.f j k) (D.f j i)) ≫ (D.t j k ≫ D.f k j)
369	      = pullback.snd (D.f i j) (D.f i k) ≫ (D.t i k ≫ D.f k i) := by
370	  have key : pullback.fst (D.f k i) (D.f k j) ≫ D.f k i
371	      = D.t' k i j ≫ pullback.snd (D.f i j) (D.f i k) ≫ D.t i k ≫ D.f k i := by
372	    rw [D.t_fac_assoc k i j, ← Category.assoc (D.t k i) (D.t i k), D.t_inv, Category.id_comp]
373	  rw [Category.assoc, ← D.t_fac_assoc j k i,
374	    ← @pullback.condition _ _ _ _ _ (D.f k i) (D.f k j) _, key, D.cocycle_assoc i j k]
375	
376	/-- **Gluing a sheaf of modules along an open cover given by a scheme glue datum**
377	(`def:scheme_modules_glue`). From a glue datum `D`, per-chart sheaves of modules `M i`,
378	and transition isomorphisms `g i j` comparing the two charts' sheaves over the overlap
379	`V (i,j)` (after pullback), produces a glued sheaf of `O_{D.glued}`-modules.
380	
381	Project-local: Mathlib has no module descent over a scheme glue datum. -/
382	noncomputable def glue (D : Scheme.GlueData)
383	    (M : ∀ i, (D.U i).Modules)
384	    (g : ∀ i j, (Scheme.Modules.pullback (D.f i j)).obj (M i) ≅
385	        (Scheme.Modules.pullback (D.t i j ≫ D.f j i)).obj (M j))
386	    -- (C1) self-identity: over the diagonal overlap `V (i,i)` (where `t i i = 𝟙`) the
387	    -- transition isomorphism is the identity, i.e. the canonical isomorphism induced by
388	    -- `f i i = t i i ≫ f i i` (blueprint `def:scheme_modules_glue` (C1)).
389	    (_hC1 : ∀ i, g i i = eqToIso (congrArg (fun φ => (Scheme.Modules.pullback φ).obj (M i))
390	        (show D.f i i = D.t i i ≫ D.f i i by rw [D.t_id i, Category.id_comp])))
391	    -- (C2) triple-overlap multiplicativity: over each triple overlap
392	    -- `V_ijk = V_ij ×_{U_i} V_ik` the base-change transports
393	    -- (`pullbackBaseChangeTransport`) of the three transition isomorphisms `g_ij`, `g_jk`,
394	    -- `g_ik` satisfy `ĝ_jk ∘ ĝ_ij = ĝ_ik`. The three `glueData_bridge_*` identities, applied
395	    -- through `pullbackCongr`, line up the endpoints so the equation is well typed
396	    -- (blueprint `def:scheme_modules_glue` (C2), `lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport`).
397	    (_hC2 : ∀ i j k,
398	        pullbackBaseChangeTransport (pullback.fst (D.f i j) (D.f i k))
399	            (D.f i j) (D.t i j ≫ D.f j i) (g i j) ≪≫
400	          (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr (glueData_bridge_mid D i j k)).app (M j) ≪≫
401	          pullbackBaseChangeTransport (D.t' i j k ≫ pullback.fst (D.f j k) (D.f j i))
402	            (D.f j k) (D.t j k ≫ D.f k j) (g j k) ≪≫
403	          (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr (glueData_bridge_tgt D i j k)).app (M k)
404	        = (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr (glueData_bridge_src D i j k)).app (M i) ≪≫
405	          pullbackBaseChangeTransport (pullback.snd (D.f i j) (D.f i k))
406	            (D.f i k) (D.t i k ≫ D.f k i) (g i k)) :
407	    D.glued.Modules :=
408	  -- **Effective descent as an equalizer of pushforwards.** The glued sheaf is the
409	  -- equalizer of the two canonical maps `∏ᵢ (ιᵢ)_* Mᵢ ⇉ ∏_{ij} (j_ij)_* (f_ij^* Mᵢ)`
410	  -- (`j_ij = f_ij ≫ ιᵢ : V_ij ↪ X`): the first map restricts the `i`-th chart section to
411	  -- `V_ij`, the second restricts the `j`-th and transports it across the transition `g_ij`,
412	  -- using the glue condition `(t_ij ≫ f_ji) ≫ ιⱼ = f_ij ≫ ιᵢ`. The cocycle hypotheses
413	  -- `_hC1`/`_hC2` are not needed to *construct* the object (they pin down the chart
414	  -- restriction isomorphisms `glueRestrictionIso`, built downstream). Pushforward preserves
415	  -- the sheaf condition and limits, so this equalizer of sheaves of modules is again a sheaf.
416	  let Qfun : D.J × D.J → D.glued.Modules := fun p =>
417	    (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (D.f p.1 p.2 ≫ D.ι p.1)).obj
418	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (D.f p.1 p.2)).obj (M p.1))
419	  let P : D.glued.Modules := ∏ᶜ fun i => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (D.ι i)).obj (M i)
420	  -- first leg: restrict the `p.1`-chart section to the overlap `V (p.1, p.2)`
421	  let aComp : ∀ p : D.J × D.J,
422	      (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (D.ι p.1)).obj (M p.1) ⟶ Qfun p := fun p =>
423	    (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (D.ι p.1)).map
424	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (D.f p.1 p.2)).unit.app (M p.1)) ≫
425	      (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp (D.f p.1 p.2) (D.ι p.1)).hom.app
426	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (D.f p.1 p.2)).obj (M p.1))
427	  -- second leg: restrict the `p.2`-chart section, transport it across `g`, and reindex
428	  -- the immersion via the glue condition
429	  let bComp : ∀ p : D.J × D.J,
430	      (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (D.ι p.2)).obj (M p.2) ⟶ Qfun p := fun p =>
431	    (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (D.ι p.2)).map
432	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction
433	          (D.t p.1 p.2 ≫ D.f p.2 p.1)).unit.app (M p.2)) ≫
434	      (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp (D.t p.1 p.2 ≫ D.f p.2 p.1) (D.ι p.2)).hom.app
435	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (D.t p.1 p.2 ≫ D.f p.2 p.1)).obj (M p.2)) ≫
436	      (Scheme.Modules.pushforward
437	        ((D.t p.1 p.2 ≫ D.f p.2 p.1) ≫ D.ι p.2)).map (g p.1 p.2).inv ≫
438	      (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardCongr
439	        (show (D.t p.1 p.2 ≫ D.f p.2 p.1) ≫ D.ι p.2 = D.f p.1 p.2 ≫ D.ι p.1 by
440	          rw [Category.assoc]; exact D.glue_condition p.1 p.2)).hom.app
441	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (D.f p.1 p.2)).obj (M p.1))
442	  let a : P ⟶ ∏ᶜ Qfun := Pi.lift fun p => Pi.π _ p.1 ≫ aComp p
443	  let b : P ⟶ ∏ᶜ Qfun := Pi.lift fun p => Pi.π _ p.2 ≫ bComp p
444	  equalizer a b
445	
446	/-! ### Pullback of free sheaves along an arbitrary scheme morphism
447	
448	The functor of points (`AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor`) acts on morphisms by
449	pullback, and for that one needs `f^* (O^n) ≅ O^n` for an *arbitrary* scheme morphism
450	`f`. Mathlib's `SheafOfModules.pullbackObjFreeIso` supplies this only when the underlying
451	site functor `Opens.map f.base` is `Final`; the previous chapters discharged that `Final`
452	hypothesis only for open immersions and isomorphisms. The first lemma below removes the
453	restriction entirely: `Opens.map f.base` is `Final` for *every* scheme morphism, because
454	the structured-arrow category over any open `V` has a terminal object (the whole space
455	`⊤`). With that in hand, `pullbackFreeIso` and `pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank` hold for all
456	morphisms. -/
457	
458	/-- For an arbitrary scheme morphism `φ`, the site functor `Opens.map φ.base` is `Final`:
459	over any open `V` of the target the structured-arrow category `{U : V ≤ φ⁻¹ U}` has the
460	terminal object `U = ⊤`, hence is connected. This is the missing ingredient that makes
461	`SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit`/`pullbackObjFreeIso` applicable to *every* morphism,
462	not just to open immersions. Project-local. -/
463	lemma opensMap_final {T' T : Scheme.{u}} (φ : T' ⟶ T) :
464	    (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map φ.base).Final := by
465	  constructor
466	  intro V
467	  set top : StructuredArrow V (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map φ.base) :=
468	    StructuredArrow.mk (Y := (⊤ : T.Opens)) (homOfLE le_top)
469	  haveI : Nonempty (StructuredArrow V (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map φ.base)) := ⟨top⟩
470	  apply zigzag_isConnected
471	  intro s t
472	  have hs : s ⟶ top := StructuredArrow.homMk (homOfLE le_top) (Subsingleton.elim _ _)
473	  have ht : t ⟶ top := StructuredArrow.homMk (homOfLE le_top) (Subsingleton.elim _ _)
474	  exact Relation.ReflTransGen.trans
475	    (Relation.ReflTransGen.single (Or.inl ⟨hs⟩))
476	    (Relation.ReflTransGen.single (Or.inr ⟨ht⟩))
477	
478	/-- **Pullback of a free sheaf of modules is free, for any scheme morphism**: for
479	`φ : T' ⟶ T` and an index type `I`, `φ^*(O_T^{⊕I}) ≅ O_{T'}^{⊕I}`. Built from
480	`SheafOfModules.pullbackObjFreeIso` once `opensMap_final` supplies the `Final` instance.
481	Project-local. -/
482	noncomputable def pullbackFreeIso {T' T : Scheme.{u}} (φ : T' ⟶ T) (I : Type u) :
483	    (Scheme.Modules.pullback φ).obj (SheafOfModules.free (R := T.ringCatSheaf) I)
484	      ≅ SheafOfModules.free (R := T'.ringCatSheaf) I := by
485	  haveI := opensMap_final φ
486	  exact SheafOfModules.pullbackObjFreeIso φ.toRingCatSheafHom I
487	
488	/-- The free-pullback comparison is natural in the base morphism: equal morphisms give
489	`pullbackFreeIso`s related by the `eqToHom` transport of their (differing) sources.
490	Project-local — used for the bundle-transition self-identity. -/
491	lemma pullbackFreeIso_eqToHom {T' T : Scheme.{u}} {φ ψ : T' ⟶ T} (h : φ = ψ) (I : Type u) :
492	    eqToHom (congrArg
493	        (fun α => (Scheme.Modules.pullback α).obj (SheafOfModules.free (R := T.ringCatSheaf) I)) h)
494	        ≫ (pullbackFreeIso ψ I).hom
495	      = (pullbackFreeIso φ I).hom := by
496	  subst h; simp
497	
498	/-- Iso-level free-pullback cancellation: for equal base morphisms `φ = ψ`, the composite
499	`pullbackFreeIso φ ≪≫ (pullbackFreeIso ψ).symm` is the `eqToIso` transport between the
500	(differing) pullback sources. Proved generically (`φ`, `ψ` variables, `subst`), so applying
501	it never forces the kernel to whnf a concrete immersion — the leaner replacement for the
502	`.hom`-level cast chain in `bundleTransition_self`. Project-local. -/
503	lemma pullbackFreeIso_trans_symm_eqToIso {T' T : Scheme.{u}} {φ ψ : T' ⟶ T} (h : φ = ψ)
504	    (I : Type u) :
505	    pullbackFreeIso φ I ≪≫ (pullbackFreeIso ψ I).symm
506	      = eqToIso (congrArg
507	          (fun α => (Scheme.Modules.pullback α).obj (SheafOfModules.free (R := T.ringCatSheaf) I))
508	          h) := by
509	  subst h; simp
510	
511	/-- **Pullback preserves rank-`d` local freeness.** If `M` is locally free of rank `d` on
512	`T`, then `φ^* M` is locally free of rank `d` on `T'`, for any scheme morphism `φ`. The
513	chart cover `{U i}` of `T` trivialising `M` pulls back to the cover `{φ⁻¹ U i}` of `T'`;
514	on each member the restriction of `φ^* M` is identified with the pulled-back chart-free
515	sheaf via the pseudofunctor comparison `pullbackComp`, the factorisation
516	`φ ∘ (φ⁻¹ U i).ι = (φ ∣_ U i) ≫ (U i).ι` (`morphismRestrict_ι`), and `pullbackFreeIso`.
517	Project-local. -/
518	lemma pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank {T' T : Scheme.{u}} (φ : T' ⟶ T) {M : T.Modules}
519	    {d : ℕ} (h : SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank M d) :
520	    SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank ((Scheme.Modules.pullback φ).obj M) d := by
521	  obtain ⟨ι, U, hcover, hloc⟩ := h
522	  refine ⟨ι, fun i => φ ⁻¹ᵁ (U i), Scheme.Hom.iSup_preimage_eq_top φ hcover, ?_⟩
523	  intro i
524	  obtain ⟨e⟩ := hloc i
525	  exact ⟨(Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (φ ⁻¹ᵁ (U i)).ι φ).app M ≪≫
526	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr (morphismRestrict_ι φ (U i)).symm).app M ≪≫
527	    ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (φ ∣_ (U i)) (U i).ι).app M).symm ≪≫
528	    (Scheme.Modules.pullback (φ ∣_ (U i))).mapIso e ≪≫
529	    pullbackFreeIso (φ ∣_ (U i)) (ULift.{u} (Fin d))⟩
530	
531	end AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules

535	/-! ## The GL_d bundle transition cocycle
536	
537	The universal quotient `U` is glued from the per-chart free rank-`d` sheaves `O_{U^I}^d`
538	along the bundle transitions `g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)⁻¹`, realised as matrix automorphisms via
539	`matrixToFreeIso` and conjugated to the overlap pullbacks by `pullbackFreeIso`. This section
540	constructs `bundleTransition` and proves its self-identity (C1); the triple-overlap
541	multiplicativity (C2) is the matrix cocycle of `lem:gr_cocycle` transported to the common
542	overlap by `pullbackBaseChangeTransport`/`glueData_bridge_*`. -/
543	
544	/-- The Cramer inverse of the self-minor `X^I_I` is the identity: since `X^I_I = 1`
545	(`universalMatrix_submatrix_self`) its inverse is `1`. Project-local; underlies C1. -/
546	lemma universalMinorInv_self (d r : ℕ) (I : Finset (Fin r)) (hI : I.card = d) :
547	    universalMinorInv d r I I hI hI = 1 := by
548	  have hmin : universalMinor d r I I hI hI = 1 := by
549	    rw [universalMinor, universalMatrix_submatrix_self, Matrix.map_one _ (map_zero _) (map_one _)]
550	  rw [universalMinorInv, hmin, inv_one]
551	
552	/-- The injected Cramer inverse and minor matrices over the overlap structure sheaf are
553	mutually inverse — the `GL_d` invertibility hypotheses for `matrixToFreeIso`. Project-local. -/
554	private lemma bundleMatrix_cancel (d r : ℕ) (I J : Finset (Fin r)) (hI : I.card = d)
555	    (hJ : J.card = d) :
556	    ((Scheme.ΓSpecIso
557	        (CommRingCat.of (Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J hI hJ)))).inv.hom.mapMatrix
558	        (universalMinorInv d r I J hI hJ)) *
559	      ((Scheme.ΓSpecIso
560	        (CommRingCat.of (Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J hI hJ)))).inv.hom.mapMatrix
561	        (universalMinor d r I J hI hJ)) = 1 ∧
562	    ((Scheme.ΓSpecIso
563	        (CommRingCat.of (Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J hI hJ)))).inv.hom.mapMatrix
564	        (universalMinor d r I J hI hJ)) *
565	      ((Scheme.ΓSpecIso
566	        (CommRingCat.of (Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J hI hJ)))).inv.hom.mapMatrix
567	        (universalMinorInv d r I J hI hJ)) = 1 := by
568	  refine ⟨?_, ?_⟩
569	  · rw [← map_mul, (universalMinorInv_mul_cancel d r I J hI hJ).1, map_one]
570	  · rw [← map_mul, (universalMinorInv_mul_cancel d r I J hI hJ).2, map_one]
571	
572	/-- The **bundle transition** `g_{I,J}` (`def:gr_bundleTransition`): the isomorphism of
573	sheaves of modules on the overlap `U^I_J` induced by the invertible matrix
574	`(X^I_J)⁻¹ = universalMinorInv d r I J`. It identifies the pullback of `O_{U^I}^d` along
575	`f_{IJ}` with the pullback of `O_{U^J}^d` along `t_{IJ} ≫ f_{JI}`, by conjugating the
576	matrix automorphism `matrixToFreeIso (X^I_J)⁻¹` (built like `chartQuotientMap`) by the
577	free-pullback comparisons `pullbackFreeIso`. -/
578	noncomputable def bundleTransition (d r : ℕ) (I J : Finset (Fin r))
579	    (hI : I.card = d) (hJ : J.card = d) :
580	    (Scheme.Modules.pullback (chartIncl d r I J hI hJ)).obj
581	        (SheafOfModules.free (R := (affineChart d r I).ringCatSheaf) (Fin d)) ≅
582	      (Scheme.Modules.pullback (chartTransition d r I J hI hJ ≫ chartIncl d r J I hJ hI)).obj
583	        (SheafOfModules.free (R := (affineChart d r J).ringCatSheaf) (Fin d)) :=
584	  Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso (chartIncl d r I J hI hJ) (Fin d) ≪≫
585	    matrixToFreeIso
586	      ((Scheme.ΓSpecIso
587	          (CommRingCat.of (Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J hI hJ)))).inv.hom.mapMatrix
588	        (universalMinorInv d r I J hI hJ))
589	      ((Scheme.ΓSpecIso
590	          (CommRingCat.of (Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J hI hJ)))).inv.hom.mapMatrix
591	        (universalMinor d r I J hI hJ))
592	      (bundleMatrix_cancel d r I J hI hJ).1
593	      (bundleMatrix_cancel d r I J hI hJ).2 ≪≫
594	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso
595	      (chartTransition d r I J hI hJ ≫ chartIncl d r J I hJ hI) (Fin d)).symm
596	
597	/-- **Self-identity of the bundle transition (C1)** (`lem:gr_bundleCocycle_id`): on the
598	diagonal overlap `U^I_I` (where `t_{II} = 𝟙`) the bundle transition is the identity, in the
599	form required by the gluing primitive `Scheme.Modules.glue`. The matrix part is the identity
600	since `(X^I_I)⁻¹ = 1` (`universalMinorInv_self`), so `matrixEnd 1 = 𝟙` (`matrixEnd_one`); the
601	two free-pullback comparisons then cancel into the `eqToIso` transport.
602	
603	Resource note (iter-060): the former `set_option maxHeartbeats 1000000 in` override is
604	removed and the proof rebuilt as a *leaner term* that the kernel checks within the default
605	budget (the earlier `.hom`-level cast chain hit a `(kernel) deterministic timeout` at default
606	heartbeats and an OOM ceiling on cold builds at `1000000`). The new term works at the **iso
607	level**: the matrix automorphism is collapsed to `Iso.refl` in the lightweight single-overlap
608	context (`hB`, free sheaves only — no pullback), and the two free-pullback comparisons cancel
609	through the *generic* lemma `pullbackFreeIso_trans_symm_eqToIso` (proved by `subst` on
610	variable morphisms), so the kernel never whnfs the concrete immersions `chartIncl` /
611	`chartTransition`. -/
612	theorem bundleTransition_self (d r : ℕ) (I : Finset (Fin r)) (hI : I.card = d) :
613	    bundleTransition d r I I hI hI
614	      = eqToIso (congrArg
615	          (fun φ => (Scheme.Modules.pullback φ).obj
616	            (SheafOfModules.free (R := (affineChart d r I).ringCatSheaf) (Fin d)))
617	          (show chartIncl d r I I hI hI
618	              = chartTransition d r I I hI hI ≫ chartIncl d r I I hI hI from by
619	            rw [chartTransition_self, Category.id_comp])) := by
620	  have hφ : chartIncl d r I I hI hI
621	      = chartTransition d r I I hI hI ≫ chartIncl d r I I hI hI := by
622	    rw [chartTransition_self, Category.id_comp]
623	  -- The matrix automorphism is the identity iso: `(X^I_I)⁻¹` injects to the identity matrix,
624	  -- so its `matrixEnd` is `𝟙`. Proved here over the single overlap chart (no pullback types).
625	  have hB : matrixToFreeIso
626	        ((Scheme.ΓSpecIso
627	            (CommRingCat.of (Localization.Away (minorDet d r I I hI hI)))).inv.hom.mapMatrix
628	          (universalMinorInv d r I I hI hI))
629	        ((Scheme.ΓSpecIso
630	            (CommRingCat.of (Localization.Away (minorDet d r I I hI hI)))).inv.hom.mapMatrix
631	          (universalMinor d r I I hI hI))
632	        (bundleMatrix_cancel d r I I hI hI).1
633	        (bundleMatrix_cancel d r I I hI hI).2
634	      = Iso.refl _ := by
635	    apply Iso.ext
636	    rw [matrixToFreeIso_hom, Iso.refl_hom, universalMinorInv_self, map_one, matrixEnd_one]
637	  -- Unfold the transition, collapse the identity matrix factor (`erw` to bridge the
638	  -- `chartOverlap`/`Spec` defeq on the inferred base scheme), and cancel the comparisons.
639	  simp only [bundleTransition]
640	  erw [hB, Iso.refl_trans]
641	  exact Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_trans_symm_eqToIso hφ (Fin d)
642	
643	/-- The bundle transition data packaged over the Grassmannian glue datum, ready to feed the
644	gluing primitive `Scheme.Modules.glue`. Project-local. -/
645	noncomputable def bundleTransitionData (d r : ℕ) :
646	    ∀ (I J : (theGlueData d r).J),
647	      (Scheme.Modules.pullback ((theGlueData d r).f I J)).obj
648	          (SheafOfModules.free (R := ((theGlueData d r).U I).ringCatSheaf) (Fin d)) ≅
649	        (Scheme.Modules.pullback ((theGlueData d r).t I J ≫ (theGlueData d r).f J I)).obj
650	          (SheafOfModules.free (R := ((theGlueData d r).U J).ringCatSheaf) (Fin d)) :=
651	  fun I J => bundleTransition d r I.1 J.1 I.2 J.2
652	
653	/-- **Triple-overlap multiplicativity of the bundle transition (C2)**
654	(`lem:gr_bundleCocycle_mul`): over each triple overlap the base-change transports of the
655	three bundle transitions satisfy `ĝ_{JK} ∘ ĝ_{IJ} = ĝ_{IK}`, in the form required by
656	`Scheme.Modules.glue`. At the matrix level this is the Cramer-inverse cocycle
657	`(X^J_K)⁻¹ (X^I_J)⁻¹ = (X^I_K)⁻¹` (`lem:gr_cocycle`, `universalMinorInv`); the substantive
658	work is transporting the three transitions to the common triple overlap via
659	`pullbackBaseChangeTransport` and aligning endpoints with `glueData_bridge_*`.
660	
661	NOTE (scaffold): the body is the remaining hard step of the bundle cocycle (~50-100 LOC of
662	matrix-to-module transport bookkeeping); the signature is the exact `_hC2` hypothesis of
663	`Scheme.Modules.glue` instantiated at `theGlueData d r` and `bundleTransitionData`. -/
664	theorem bundleTransition_cocycle (d r : ℕ) (I J K : (theGlueData d r).J) :
665	    Scheme.Modules.pullbackBaseChangeTransport
666	        (Limits.pullback.fst ((theGlueData d r).f I J) ((theGlueData d r).f I K))
667	        ((theGlueData d r).f I J) ((theGlueData d r).t I J ≫ (theGlueData d r).f J I)
668	        (bundleTransitionData d r I J) ≪≫
669	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr
670	          (Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_mid (theGlueData d r) I J K)).app _ ≪≫
671	      Scheme.Modules.pullbackBaseChangeTransport
672	        ((theGlueData d r).t' I J K ≫
673	          Limits.pullback.fst ((theGlueData d r).f J K) ((theGlueData d r).f J I))
674	        ((theGlueData d r).f J K) ((theGlueData d r).t J K ≫ (theGlueData d r).f K J)
675	        (bundleTransitionData d r J K) ≪≫
676	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr
677	          (Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_tgt (theGlueData d r) I J K)).app _
678	    = (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr
679	          (Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_src (theGlueData d r) I J K)).app _ ≪≫
680	      Scheme.Modules.pullbackBaseChangeTransport
681	        (Limits.pullback.snd ((theGlueData d r).f I J) ((theGlueData d r).f I K))
682	        ((theGlueData d r).f I K) ((theGlueData d r).t I K ≫ (theGlueData d r).f K I)
683	        (bundleTransitionData d r I K) := by

717	/-! ## The functor of points and the universal property
718	
719	The Grassmannian functor sends a scheme `T` to the set of *equivalence classes* of
720	rank-`d` quotients `q : O_T^r ↠ F` with `F` locally free of rank `d`. We encode a single
721	such quotient as the structure `RankQuotient r d T`, the equivalence as `RankQuotient.Rel`
722	(an isomorphism of the targets commuting with the quotient maps), and the functor's value
723	at `T` as the quotient `Quotient (rqSetoid r d T)`. The pullback action `rqPullback`
724	together with `pullbackFreeIso`/`pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank` (and the fact that the
725	pullback functor preserves epimorphisms, being a left adjoint) realise functoriality.
726	
727	Because a sheaf of modules `F : T.Modules` is a large object, this quotient lives in
728	`Type 1` (not `Type 0` as the original scaffold signature stated); the corrected universe
729	is the only change to the pinned signature, and it is forced — the substantive content is
730	unchanged. -/
731	
732	/-- A **rank-`d` quotient of `O_T^r`** on a scheme `T`: a sheaf of modules `F` on `T`,
733	locally free of rank `d`, together with an epimorphism `q : O_T^r ↠ F`. This is the
734	unbundled datum whose equivalence classes form the value of the Grassmannian functor. -/
735	structure RankQuotient (r d : ℕ) (T : Scheme.{0}) where
736	  /-- The quotient sheaf. -/
737	  F : T.Modules
738	  /-- The quotient map out of the trivial rank-`r` bundle. -/
739	  q : SheafOfModules.free (R := T.ringCatSheaf) (Fin r) ⟶ F
740	  /-- The quotient map is an epimorphism (surjective). -/
741	  epi : Epi q
742	  /-- The quotient sheaf is locally free of rank `d`. -/
743	  locFree : SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank F d
744	
745	/-- Two rank-`d` quotients are **equivalent** when there is an isomorphism of the targets
746	commuting with the quotient maps (equivalently, when the kernels of the quotient maps
747	coincide). -/
748	def RankQuotient.Rel {r d : ℕ} {T : Scheme.{0}} (x y : RankQuotient r d T) : Prop :=
749	  ∃ f : x.F ≅ y.F, x.q ≫ f.hom = y.q
750	
751	lemma RankQuotient.rel_refl {r d : ℕ} {T : Scheme.{0}} (x : RankQuotient r d T) : x.Rel x :=
752	  ⟨Iso.refl _, Category.comp_id _⟩
753	
754	lemma RankQuotient.rel_symm {r d : ℕ} {T : Scheme.{0}} {x y : RankQuotient r d T}
755	    (h : x.Rel y) : y.Rel x := by
756	  obtain ⟨f, hf⟩ := h
757	  exact ⟨f.symm, by rw [Iso.symm_hom, Iso.comp_inv_eq]; exact hf.symm⟩
758	
759	lemma RankQuotient.rel_trans {r d : ℕ} {T : Scheme.{0}} {x y z : RankQuotient r d T}
760	    (h1 : x.Rel y) (h2 : y.Rel z) : x.Rel z := by
761	  obtain ⟨f, hf⟩ := h1; obtain ⟨g, hg⟩ := h2
762	  -- term-mode (the `T.Modules` def-diamond blocks positional category `rw`)
763	  exact ⟨f ≪≫ g,
764	    (congrArg (x.q ≫ ·) (Iso.trans_hom f g)).trans <|
765	      (Category.assoc x.q f.hom g.hom).symm.trans <|
766	        (congrArg (· ≫ g.hom) hf).trans hg⟩
767	
768	/-- The equivalence-of-quotients setoid on `RankQuotient r d T`. -/
769	instance rqSetoid (r d : ℕ) (T : Scheme.{0}) : Setoid (RankQuotient r d T) where
770	  r := RankQuotient.Rel
771	  iseqv := ⟨RankQuotient.rel_refl, RankQuotient.rel_symm, RankQuotient.rel_trans⟩
772	
773	/-- The **pullback action** on a rank-`d` quotient: pull the target sheaf and quotient map
774	back along `ψ`, re-presenting the source as the trivial bundle via `pullbackFreeIso`. The
775	result is again an epimorphism (pullback preserves epis) onto a rank-`d` locally free sheaf
776	(`pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank`). -/
777	noncomputable def rqPullback {r d : ℕ} {T' T : Scheme.{0}} (ψ : T' ⟶ T)
778	    (x : RankQuotient r d T) : RankQuotient r d T' where
779	  F := (Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).obj x.F
780	  q := (Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso ψ (Fin r)).inv ≫ (Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).map x.q
781	  epi :=
782	    -- fully explicit: the def-diamond on `T.Modules` blocks `Epi`-instance search, so
783	    -- `x.epi` is threaded through `map_epi`/`epi_comp` by hand
784	    @CategoryTheory.epi_comp _ _ _ _ _
785	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso ψ (Fin r)).inv inferInstance
786	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).map x.q)
787	      (@CategoryTheory.Functor.map_epi _ _ _ _ (Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ) inferInstance _ _
788	        x.q x.epi)
789	  locFree := Scheme.Modules.pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank ψ x.locFree
790	
791	/-- The pullback action respects the equivalence relation, hence descends to quotients. -/
792	lemma rqPullback_rel {r d : ℕ} {T' T : Scheme.{0}} (ψ : T' ⟶ T)
793	    {x y : RankQuotient r d T} (h : x.Rel y) :
794	    (rqPullback ψ x).Rel (rqPullback ψ y) := by
795	  obtain ⟨f, hf⟩ := h
796	  refine ⟨(Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).mapIso f, ?_⟩
797	  change ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso ψ (Fin r)).inv ≫ (Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).map x.q) ≫
798	      (Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).map f.hom
799	    = (Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso ψ (Fin r)).inv ≫ (Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).map y.q
800	  rw [Category.assoc, ← (Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).map_comp]
801	  exact congrArg
802	    (fun m => (Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso ψ (Fin r)).inv ≫ (Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).map m) hf
803	
804	end AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian

11	/-!
12	# Section graded ring infrastructure, Layer 1: tensor powers of a sheaf of modules
13	
14	This file builds the Mathlib-absent infrastructure of
15	`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_SectionGradedRing.tex`, Layer 1
16	(`sec:sgr_tensor_powers`): the tensor product, tensor powers, and twists of
17	sheaves of modules over a scheme `X`, together with the unitor and braiding
18	isomorphisms of the sheaf tensor product.
19	
20	The category `X.Modules = SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf` of sheaves of modules
21	over a scheme carries **no** monoidal structure in Mathlib (the structure sheaf
22	varies the base ring over opens).  Mathlib *does* supply:
23	
24	* the symmetric monoidal structure on the category of **presheaves** of modules
25	  `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`
26	  (`Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal`), and
27	* the sheafification functor `PresheafOfModules.sheafification`
28	  (`Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification`).
29	
30	We therefore build the tensor product of sheaves of modules as the sheafification
31	of the objectwise (presheaf) tensor product, following
32	[Stacks, Tag 01CA].
33	
34	## Main definitions
35	
36	* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafification` — the scheme-level
37	  sheafification functor `X.PresheafOfModules ⥤ X.Modules`.
38	* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj` (`def:sheafTensorObj`) —
39	  `F ⊗ G := (F.toPresheaf ⊗ G.toPresheaf)^#`.
40	* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow` (`def:sheafTensorPow`) —
41	  the `m`-th tensor power `L^{⊗m}` of a sheaf of modules.
42	* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow` (`def:sheafModuleTwist`) —
43	  the `m`-twist `F(m) = F ⊗ L^{⊗m}`.
44	* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafificationCounitIso` — the reflective
45	  counit iso `(F.toPresheaf)^# ≅ F`.
46	* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjUnitIso`,
47	  `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjRightUnitor`,
48	  `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorBraiding` — the left/right unitor and
49	  braiding isomorphisms of the sheaf tensor product.
50	
51	The comparison isomorphism `L^{⊗m} ⊗ L^{⊗m'} ≅ L^{⊗(m+m')}`
52	(`lem:sheafTensorPow_add`) is **deferred**: see the handoff note before the end
53	of the file for the single missing ingredient (strong-monoidality of the module
54	sheafification functor) and the launching pad assembled here.
55	-/
56	
57	universe u
58	
59	open CategoryTheory MonoidalCategory Limits
60	
61	namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules
62	
63	variable {X : Scheme.{u}}
64	
65	/-- The scheme-level sheafification functor, sending a presheaf of modules over a
66	scheme `X` to its associated sheaf of modules `X.Modules`.  It is the
67	`PresheafOfModules.sheafification` functor for the identity morphism of the
68	underlying presheaf of rings (which is locally bijective).  Non-private because it
69	appears in the statement of `isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit`. -/
70	noncomputable def sheafification : X.PresheafOfModules ⥤ X.Modules :=
71	  PresheafOfModules.sheafification (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj)
72	
73	/-- The category `X.PresheafOfModules` of presheaves of modules over a scheme,
74	presented in the exact form `PresheafOfModules (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)`
75	for which Mathlib equips it with a symmetric monoidal structure.  This is
76	*definitionally* `X.PresheafOfModules` (since
77	`X.ringCatSheaf.obj = X.sheaf.obj ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat`), so a term of
78	either type is accepted for the other. -/
79	private abbrev MonoidalPresheaf (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type _ :=
80	  _root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (X.sheaf.obj ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)
81	
82	/-- The tensor product of two sheaves of modules over a scheme, defined as the
83	sheafification of the objectwise tensor product presheaf
84	(Mathlib `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`).  See [Stacks, Tag 01CA]
85	(`def:sheafTensorObj`). -/
86	noncomputable def tensorObj (F G : X.Modules) : X.Modules :=
87	  sheafification.obj
88	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorObj (C := MonoidalPresheaf X)
89	      ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj F) ((toPresheafOfModules X).obj G))
90	
91	/-- The structure sheaf as a sheaf of modules over itself: the unit object of the
92	tensor product, i.e. the zeroth tensor power `L^{⊗0} = 𝒪_X`
93	(`lem:moduleUnit_mathlib`). -/
94	private noncomputable abbrev unitModule (X : Scheme.{u}) : X.Modules :=
95	  SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf
96	
97	/-- The `m`-th tensor power `L^{⊗m}` of a sheaf of modules over a scheme, defined
98	by recursion: `L^{⊗0} = 𝒪_X` (the unit module) and
99	`L^{⊗(m+1)} = L^{⊗m} ⊗ L`.  See [Stacks, Tag 01CU] (`def:sheafTensorPow`). -/
100	noncomputable def tensorPow (L : X.Modules) : ℕ → X.Modules
101	  | 0 => unitModule X
102	  | (m + 1) => tensorObj (tensorPow L m) L
103	
104	@[simp] private lemma tensorPow_zero (L : X.Modules) : tensorPow L 0 = unitModule X := rfl
105	
106	@[simp] private lemma tensorPow_succ (L : X.Modules) (m : ℕ) :
107	    tensorPow L (m + 1) = tensorObj (tensorPow L m) L := rfl
108	
109	/-- The `m`-twist `F(m) = F ⊗ L^{⊗m}` of a sheaf of modules `F` by the `m`-th
110	tensor power of a line bundle `L` (`def:sheafModuleTwist`).  This is the
111	degree-`m` carrier of the section graded module. -/
112	noncomputable def moduleTensorPow (F L : X.Modules) (m : ℕ) : X.Modules :=
113	  tensorObj F (tensorPow L m)
114	
115	@[simp] private lemma moduleTensorPow_zero (F L : X.Modules) :
116	    moduleTensorPow F L 0 = tensorObj F (unitModule X) := rfl
